gsm_01.jpg: A professional timeline infographic illustrating the evolution of cellular network generations from 1G to 4G. The horizontal timeline spans from the early 1980s to the 2010s, with four clearly labeled milestones: '1G Analog (NMT/AMPS)' in the 1980s, '2G GSM (Digital)' in the 1990s, '3G UMTS (Multimedia)' in the early 2000s, and '4G LTE (All-IP)' in the 2010s. For each milestone, a representative icon is shown: an analog car phone for 1G, a classic Nokia phone for 2G, an early smartphone for 3G, and a modern data modem for 4G. Key breakthrough features are annotated along the timeline: 'Voice Only' for 1G, 'SMS, Encryption' for 2G, 'Internet, Video Calls' for 3G, and 'All-IP Packet Network' for 4G. The visual style is strictly technical and professional, using clean vector graphics and a dark blue and silver color palette. No abstract or decorative elements are included. The background is neutral white for maximum clarity. All text is in English. The composition is balanced and serves as a definitive introduction to mobile network generations, emphasizing that 2G GSM is the foundation for all subsequent digital systems.
gsm_02.jpg: A professional and historically accurate technical illustration of a 1G NMT 450 analog car phone system from the 1980s. The central focus is a large car-mounted telephone handset with a coiled cord, installed in a vintage vehicle interior, connected to a bulky transceiver unit in the trunk. Callout lines identify the 'FM Radio Transceiver Unit', the 'Control Head / Handset', and the 'Roof-Mounted Antenna'. A side panel shows the '450 MHz Frequency Band' labeled as 'FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)' with analog voice channels. A technical note box highlights key flaws: 'No Encryption – Conversations Eavesdroppable with a Regular Radio' and 'No Compatibility Between Countries'. The style is strictly academic and realistic, using clean technical line-art illustration. No abstract or non-technical elements are included. The color palette uses professional grays, blues, and period-appropriate earth tones for the hardware. All text is in English. The illustration proves that 1G demonstrated the demand for mobile communication, which forced the creation of the digital GSM standard.
gsm_03.jpg: A professional illustration celebrating the GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) standard as the world's first digital cellular revolution. The image features a large world map with GSM coverage areas highlighted in a professional blue, showing intercontinental reach. A central rendering of an iconic 1990s mobile phone, such as a Nokia 2110, is displayed with its SIM card slot highlighted. Technical highlights callouts list: 'Digital Transmission (TDMA/FDMA) – Voice Converted to Bits', 'SIM Card Authentication & A5 Encryption Algorithm', 'International Roaming – Same Phone in Different Countries', and 'Short Message Service (SMS) – A New Communication Channel'. A small diagram shows the SMS message pathway from phone to SMSC to recipient. Logos of early European GSM operators are subtly displayed to show multinational adoption. The style is strictly technical and professional, using clean vector graphics. No abstract or decorative elements are used. The background is neutral white. All text is in English, demonstrating GSM as the most popular mobile standard in history.
gsm_04.jpg: A detailed technical block diagram of the overall GSM network architecture divided into three main subsystems. The 'BSS (Base Station Subsystem)' block contains a BTS tower icon and a BSC server icon connected by an 'A-bis' interface line. The 'NSS (Network and Switching Subsystem)' block contains labeled nodes for 'MSC (Mobile Switching Center)', 'HLR (Home Location Register)', and 'VLR (Visitor Location Register)', connected by internal lines. The 'OSS (Operation Support Subsystem)' block shows a monitoring workstation and technician dashboard icon. Logical interface lines connect all three subsystems, labeled with their standard names. A callout box explains the analogy: 'BSS is the radio hands (towers and controllers), NSS is the network brain (switching and databases), OSS is the management eyes (monitoring and operations)'. The style is strictly academic and textbook-like, using clean 2D geometric blocks and professional typography. No abstract or decorative elements are included. The background is neutral white. The color scheme uses professional tech blues, silver, and white. All labeling is in English, providing an expert-level architectural overview explaining that each subsystem handles a different stage of the connection.
gsm_05.jpg: A technical architecture diagram showing the functional hierarchy of the GSM BSS (Base Station Subsystem). The diagram illustrates the signal path from a 'Mobile Station (MS)' represented by a phone icon on the left, to a 'BTS (Base Transceiver Station)' represented by a cell tower icon in the middle, to a 'BSC (Base Station Controller)' represented by a server rack icon on the right. Interface labels are clearly marked: 'Um (Air Interface)' between MS and BTS, and 'A-bis' between BTS and BSC. A prominent callout highlights that one BSC can control '50-100 BTS units', emphasizing the centralization of control logic. The BTS block is labeled 'Radio Only – Handles Transmission Tasks', indicating it performs only radio functions. The BSC block is labeled 'Intelligence – Handover Decision Logic & Radio Resource Management', showing it contains the control intelligence. The style is strictly academic and professional, using clean vector graphics and clear technical labeling in English. No decorative or abstract elements are present. The background is neutral white. The color scheme uses professional tech blues and whites. This illustration serves as a definitive guide to the BSS hierarchy, clearly showing that the BTS is 'dumb' while the BSC contains the intelligence for handovers and resource allocation.
